vendor: Database technology supporting 5+ gigabytes of in-system main memory enabling high-performance data access by eliminating I/O bottlenecks
lifecycle_at_study: Evolved into modern in-memory databases (SAP HANA
notes: MemSQL/SingleStore), 1994, , SAP HANA; Oracle TimesTen; Redis; SingleStore

vendor: CPU architecture allowing addressable memory far beyond the 2GB limit of 32-bit; foundation for LIMD technology
lifecycle_at_study: 64-bit is now universal in server and desktop computing; 32-bit largely obsolete for servers

vendor: Digital Equipment Corporation's Very Large Memory 64-bit architecture for Alpha servers enabling tens of gigabytes of addressable RAM
lifecycle_at_study: Proprietary to DEC/Compaq; became obsolete after HP absorbed DEC; replaced by x86-64 platforms

vendor: DEC's 64-bit RISC processor powering the Alpha 8200 and 8400 server lines; first major commercial 64-bit server CPU
lifecycle_at_study: Discontinued after HP acquisition of Compaq; last Alpha CPUs shipped ~2007

vendor: SGI's 64-bit MIPS-based processor; 64-bit but limited to 2GB per process in 1995
lifecycle_at_study: MIPS processors for commercial servers largely obsolete; MIPS IP discontinued by MIPS Technologies 2021

vendor: Sun Microsystems' 64-bit UltraSPARC processor and SunOS adaptation for 64-bit applications
lifecycle_at_study: SPARC architecture discontinued by Oracle post-2010 Sun acquisition; last SPARC server shipped 2017

vendor: HP's 32/64-bit PA-RISC processor planned for HP 9000 Enterprise Parallel Servers; successor PA-8000 was 64-bit
lifecycle_at_study: HP PA-RISC discontinued; replaced by Itanium then x86-64

vendor: IBM and Motorola's planned 64-bit PowerPC chipset for upgrading existing PowerPC architectures
lifecycle_at_study: IBM POWER architecture continues as POWER9/POWER10; used in IBM mainframes and HPC; PowerPC in consumer devices largely replaced by ARM

vendor: Oracle's relational database versions 7.1/7.2/7.3 rearchitected for 64-bit LIMD support with parallel scalability and symmetric data replication
lifecycle_at_study: Oracle 7.x superseded; Oracle database continues as 19c/21c/23c

vendor: Sybase's RDBMS with Named Caches (up to 2GB per cache) variable block I/O and symmetric network engines optimized for LIMD hardware
lifecycle_at_study: System 11 superseded by later Sybase/SAP versions; SAP ASE (Adaptive Server Enterprise) is current successor

vendor: Informix parallel RDBMS with 64-bit support for LIMD hardware; 8.0 in beta in 1995 with strong data-warehousing performance
lifecycle_at_study: Acquired by IBM 2001; continues as IBM Informix 14.x

vendor: Multi-processor architecture sharing memory and OS; dominant scale-up approach for database servers in the 1990s
lifecycle_at_study: SMP is still the dominant architecture for multi-core server CPUs; continues in modern server platforms
notes: Multi-core x86-64; AMD EPYC; Intel Xeon

vendor: Architecture with many independent nodes each with own memory connected by high-speed network; used for extreme-scale database queries
lifecycle_at_study: MPP concepts continue in modern big data platforms (Teradata; AWS Redshift; Snowflake)

vendor: Transaction Processing Performance Council benchmark measuring OLTP throughput in transactions per minute (tpmC)
lifecycle_at_study: TPC-C still actively used as of 2024; benchmark standard for OLTP database performance comparisons

vendor: Relational database originally developed by DEC; acquired by Oracle; optimized for VAX/VMS and Alpha environments
lifecycle_at_study: Oracle Rdb continues in niche use on OpenVMS; current version Oracle Rdb 7.4

vendor: Sybase component delivering order-of-magnitude speedup for complex queries using columnar storage techniques
lifecycle_at_study: Evolved into SAP IQ (formerly Sybase IQ); still active as SAP IQ for analytics

vendor: Digital's 100 MB/s cluster interconnect bus allowing in-memory data passing between cluster nodes without disk I/O
lifecycle_at_study: Proprietary DEC interconnect; replaced by InfiniBand and high-speed Ethernet for cluster communications

vendor: High-speed networking technology enabling gigabyte-per-second bandwidth requiring fast in-memory buffering
lifecycle_at_study: ATM largely replaced by Ethernet (10GbE; 100GbE) and MPLS in carrier networks

vendor: CPU architecture limiting addressable memory to ~2-4GB; the dominant architecture being superseded by 64-bit in 1995
lifecycle_at_study: 32-bit server computing largely obsolete by mid-2000s; 32-bit client computing obsolete by 2020s

vendor: Tandem's fault-tolerant RDBMS for its Himalaya line designed for high-availability OLTP without LIMD support in 1995
lifecycle_at_study: Continues as HPE NonStop SQL / NonStop Advanced Server; still used in financial OLTP
